title: Space Tech Briefing — July 02, 2026
summary:
Good morning, space cadets! It's Thursday, July 02, 2026, and your daily dose of the cosmos is locked, loaded, and ready for launch! I'm your host, bringing you the latest and greatest from the world of space technology. Let's dive right in!

First up, breaking news straight from the labs preparing for our future in the stars. A brand new study, dubbed BRAHMS, is underway! Ten dedicated volunteers are now participating in a critical research project. They're exploring the combined effects of weightlessness and severe caloric restriction. This vital work is all about understanding the human body's limits and adapting it for the long-duration missions of tomorrow, paving the way for deep space exploration. Incredible science happening right now!

And speaking of critical infrastructure, we've got an update on SSALTO – the Altimetry, Orbitography, and Precise Localization Ground Segment. This isn't just a fancy name; it's the backbone for incredibly accurate satellite operations. SSALTO is crucial for tracking our Earth observation satellites, meticulously mapping orbits, and delivering pinpoint localization data. Think global navigation, climate monitoring, and understanding our planet's changing surface – SSALTO makes it all possible, ensuring our eyes in the sky are always precisely where they need to be.

Now, for something a bit more hands-on and explosive! C'Space has just opened its doors in Ger, inviting everyone to witness the thrilling spectacle of experimental rocket launches! This isn't just for the pros; it's a fantastic event where you can watch incredible amateur rocketry in action. Plus, there's a fantastic water rocket workshop for the kids – sparking the next generation of space engineers and explorers! If you're looking for an inspiring day out, C'Space is the place to be.

And as we look back at some truly remarkable achievements from the past week, we simply *have* to talk about the European Space Agency's Euclid telescope! Just unveiled on June 24th, Euclid has delivered the largest, highest-resolution visible light photo ever taken of the Milky Way's beating heart. This isn't just a picture; it's a mind-blowing 6-gigapixel mosaic, revealing over 60 million stars in a single, awe-inspiring scene. The level of detail in this incredibly dense region of our galaxy is unprecedented. Euclid captured this masterpiece back in March 2025, during a 26-hour observation session, meticulously stitching together nine distinct views. To get those vibrant colors, Euclid's visible light data was combined with observations from the Canada-France-Hawaii Telescope. What a view!

That's all for your space tech briefing this morning! Thank you for tuning in and joining us on this incredible journey through the cosmos. Stay curious, keep looking up, and we'll catch you next time!
